segments.text:
Sample Preparation Techniques Liquid Extraction
Extraction: analyte dissolved in solvent that does not necessarily dissolve the entire sample
Process does not decompose the analyte.
Microwave-assisted extraction uses temperatures 50°–100°C higher than the boiling points of solvents at atmospheric pressure.
Pesticides dissolve but soil remains behind.
Figure 28-12

Supercritical Fluid Extraction (1 of 2)
Supercritical fluid : a fluid whose temperature is above its critical temperature and whose pressure is above its critical pressure
It has properties of both a liquid and a gas.
Supercritical fluid extraction : can be used to extract compounds (usually from solids)
CO 2 is the most common supercritical fluid (inexpensive and eliminates costly disposal of organic solvent waste).
Addition of second solvent such as methanol increases solubility of polar analytes.
Nonpolar substances can be extracted with supercritical Ar.
Some chelators can extract metal ions into supercritical CO 2 .

Supercritical Fluid Extraction (2 of 2)
Figure 28-13

Liquid-Liquid Extraction
Glassware for continuous liquid-liquid extraction of nonvolatile analyte
( a ) Extraction solvent is denser than the liquid being extracted.
( b ) Extraction solvent is lighter than the liquid being extracted.
Typically extract from aqueous solution into organic solvent
Solvent boils from flask and condenses in extraction vessel
Figure 28-14

Dispersive Liquid-Liquid Microextraction
Disperser solvent miscible in both phases
Lowers the energy of the interface between phases
Permits high-surface-area emulsion of small droplets
Mass transfer of analyte between two phases rapid because interfacial surface area so large
Figure 28-15

Solid-Supported Liquid-Liquid Extraction
Another way to reduce solvent use
Aqueous phase suspended on a microporous medium
Organic solvent passed through medium to extract analyte
Can be carried out on a 96-well plate
Figure 28-16

Cloud Point Extraction (1 of 2)
Uses surfactant to accomplish liquid-liquid extraction plus preconcentration of analyte.
Above critical micelle concentration , surfactant aggregates to form micelles.
Each micelle is a nanoscale droplet
Hydrophobic interior
Hydrophilic exterior
If the solution is heated above the cloud point temperature , the exterior of each micelle is partly dehydrated and micelles aggregate into separate phase that is denser than the aqueous solution.
Figure 28-17

Cloud Point Extraction (2 of 2)
Concentration of Iron in Beer
Iron reduced to Fe(II) by ascorbic acid
Treated with 2-(5-bromo-2-pyridylazo)-5-diethylaminophenol to form a purple complex
Hydrophobic exterior
Ligand atoms are in bold
Color Plate 36

Solid-Phase Extraction
Solid-phase extraction : preconcentration procedure in which solution is passed through a short column of chromatographic stationary phase (such as C 18 -silica)
Trace solutes adsorbed onto column can be eluted with a small volume of high mobile phase strength solvent.
Specific isolation of analytes from sample matrix.
Stationary phase can be molecularly imprinted polymers.
Uses little solvent and generates little waste.

Typical Steps in Solid-Phase Extraction
Figure 28-18

Color Plate 1: Solid-Phase Extraction
Solid-phase extraction cartridge containing 500 mg of C 18 -silica
Before loading cartridge, it was washed with 5 mL of 3 mM HCl in CH 3 OH and washed with H 2 O.
Sample is two dyes in 1 mL of H 2 O.

Sample Cleanup
Complex samples can be difficult to analyze. Solid-phase extraction cleans up the sample, simplifying the analysis.
Chromatograms of Drug Naproxen in Blood Serum
Without cleanup, serum proteins obscure naproxen signal.
Solid-phase extraction removes most of the protein.
Figure 28-19

Solid-Phase Extraction Used to Preconcentrate Cocaine from River Water
Mixed-Mode Cation-Exchange Reverse-Phase Sorbent (MCX)
Family of resins; backbone contains lipophilic benzene rings and hydrophilic pyrrolidone rings.
Four ion-exchange derivatives are useful for retaining and releasing different types of analytes.
Retention/release is controlled by changing pH, solvent, and ionic strength.
Figure 28-20

Solid Phase Extraction: 96-Well Plates
A 96-well plate can be used for multiple samples of exploratory research.
Two common styles:
Right: Syringe-like wells each contain 5–60 mg of resin.
Left: Pasteur-pipet-like wells use smaller volume and can be eluted by 25–50  L of solvent.
Figure 28-21

Solid-Phase Extraction of Gases
Apparatus for Trapping Basic or Acidic Gases by Ion Exchange
Add acid to known amount of powder in test tube to release CO 2 .
When solution is purged with N 2 , the CO 2 is captured by moist anion-exchange resin in the sidearm.
Carbonate is eluted from the resin with 1 M NaNO 3 and measured by titration with acid.
Figure 28-22
Table 28-7 Use of ion-exchange resin for trapping gases (1 of 2)
Table 28-7 Use of ion-exchange resin for trapping gases (2 of 2)

QuEChERS (“ kĕ-chers ”)
QuEChERS : sample preparation procedure that is
Qu ick
E asy
Ch eap
E ffective
R ugged
S afe
Steps: (1) Extraction of analytes followed by (2) sample cleanup to remove matrix components prior to analysis
Good recovery of many analytes.
Uses include extraction of
Pesticides/contaminants from fruits, vegetables, plants, soil, sediment
Antibiotics from meat

QuEChERS
Partial gas chromatogram time-of-flight mass spectrum of a pesticide extracted from dietary supplement
Even though sample has been “cleaned up,” is still a mixture of many components
Column: 30 m × 0.25 mm Restek Rxi-5Sil MS (0.35 μ m thick)
Main spectrum is total ion count
Inset is the m / z 312 extracted ion
Figure 28-23

Derivatization
Derivatization: procedure in which analyte is chemically modified to make it easier to detect or separate
